settings.title: Why Your Logo Needs Room to Breathe
settings.excerpt: The most common design mistake we fix is crowding, and it costs nothing to avoid.
settings.body:
When a logo looks cheap, the problem is often not the logo at all. It is what is jammed up against it.

Give a mark some empty space and it reads as confident. Crowd it with text, a border, and three social icons, and the same mark suddenly looks nervous.

The space is doing real work

That empty area around a logo is not wasted, it is part of the design. It tells the eye where to rest and gives the mark room to be itself. Strip it away and even a strong logo starts to feel like it is shouting over the things crammed beside it.

  • Keep clear space around the logo equal to the height of one letter.
  • Resist filling that space with a tagline or icons.
  • Check it at small sizes, where crowding hurts the most.

A simple rule we teach every Pixelcraft client is to keep clear space around the logo equal to the height of one letter. Try it on your own header. The difference is immediate, and it is free.

Where it goes wrong most

Headers and social profiles are the usual culprits, because space is tight and the temptation is to pack in everything at once. The logo ends up wedged between a menu and a phone number with no breathing room at all.

The fix costs nothing. Pull the surrounding elements back, give the mark its margin, and look again. Nine times out of ten the brand suddenly feels more expensive, and you have not changed the logo by a single pixel.
